source.journal_name: Romanian Journal of Information Science and Technology

paper.title: Modal Approach to the Theory of Energy Transfer Mediated by a Metallic Nanosphere
paper.abstract: Theoretical calculation of FRET enhancement factor using modal approach for metallic nanosphere. Boundary Green's function derived from spectral properties of electrostatic operator. Analytical results for spherical geometry with modal decomposition of plasmonic response.

verdict.summary: Theoretical physics paper with justified heavy formalism. Derives analytical Green's function for plasmon-mediated energy transfer. Mathematical apparatus (Eqs. 1-26) is necessary for deriving electromagnetic boundary conditions and modal decompositions. Level 4 complexity warranted.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Analytical theory of plasmon-enhanced Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) using boundary integral methods
**Level:** 4 (High)
**Rationale:** Novel theoretical derivation requiring electromagnetic boundary conditions, spectral decomposition of operators, and Green's function formalism. This is fundamental physics research, not an application.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 formal numbered definitions
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 26 (Eqs. 1-26)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~52 (26 equations x 2)
Expected range (Level 4): 30-80
Ratio: 0.65x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - The equations derive the boundary Green's function, spectral decomposition, and enhancement factors. Each equation builds toward the analytical result.

### Necessity Test
- Eq. 1 (FRET rate): Standard physics formula, necessary
- Eqs. 3-14 (Green's function derivation): Necessary for modal approach
- Eqs. 15-26 (Enhancement factors): Required for analytical predictions
- All equations are referenced and used in derivations

**Verdict:** Formalism necessary - cannot express electromagnetic theory in plain English.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

32 references. Self-citations: 8 (refs 13-15, 23-26, 29) = 25% SCR
All self-citations are directly relevant (prior work on electrostatic operators, plasmonics). No citation ring indicators.

---

## Structural Analysis

Clear derivation structure. Numerical validation section present. No limitations section but appropriate for theoretical physics. No code/data artifacts (analytical work).

---

## Conclusion

High-quality theoretical physics paper with appropriate formalism for electromagnetic theory. The mathematical apparatus is necessary for deriving analytical results. Modal decomposition approach provides genuine insight into plasmonic FRET enhancement.

paper.title: Electro-Optical Method for Surface Recombination Evaluation in Silicon Carbide Bipolar Junction Transistors
paper.abstract: Two novel methods for measuring minority carrier lifetime and surface recombination velocity in SiC BJTs. Combines current switch lifetime measurement with electro-optical excitation. Evaluated surface recombination velocity of electrons in n+p junctions.

verdict.summary: Experimental semiconductor physics paper with appropriate formalism. Equations describe standard carrier lifetime physics (Eqs. 1-19). Novel measurement methodology validated on both Si and SiC BJTs. Formalism necessary for physical models.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Novel electro-optical measurement method for minority carrier lifetime and surface recombination in SiC BJTs
**Level:** 3 (Medium)
**Rationale:** Combines existing measurement techniques in novel way. Requires semiconductor physics equations for carrier dynamics.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 formal numbered definitions
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 19 (Eqs. 1-19)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~38 (19 equations x 2)
Expected range (Level 3): 10-40
Ratio: 0.95x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - Equations describe standard semiconductor physics: error function for lifetime (Eq. 1), photocurrent generation (Eqs. 2-12), surface recombination (Eqs. 17-19).

### Necessity Test
- Eq. 1 (erf equation): Standard lifetime formula, necessary
- Eqs. 2-12 (photocurrent physics): Required for method derivation
- Eqs. 13-19 (surface recombination): Necessary for velocity calculation
- Could equations be English? NO - semiconductor physics requires mathematical relationships

**Verdict:** Formalism appropriate for experimental semiconductor physics.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

11 references. Self-citations: 1 (ref 1) = 9% SCR. Low and acceptable. References cover relevant prior work on lifetime measurement and SiC devices.

---

## Structural Analysis

Well-structured experimental paper: method presentation, calibration on Si BJTs, application to SiC BJTs. Extensive experimental data (Figs. 5-15). Limitations acknowledged implicitly in discussion of measurement uncertainty.

---

## Conclusion

Solid experimental paper presenting novel measurement methodology. Mathematical formalism is standard semiconductor physics, necessary for describing carrier dynamics. Good experimental validation on both Si (calibration) and SiC (application) devices.

paper.title: Assessing by Raman spectroscopy the quality of CVD graphene transferred on oxidized silicon and quartz substrates
paper.abstract: Raman spectroscopy study of CVD graphene transfer via electrochemical delamination to SiO2/Si and quartz substrates. Characterization of graphene quality through D, G, 2D band analysis. PMMA removal identified as critical step causing defects.

verdict.summary: Materials characterization paper with minimal but appropriate formalism. No unnecessary mathematical notation - quality ratios (I2D/IG, ID/IG) are standard Raman metrics. Focus is on experimental methodology and characterization data.

papers.provenance:

**Topic:** Materials characterization of graphene transfer quality using Raman spectroscopy
**Level:** 2 (Low)

### Proportionality

### Necessity Test

---

## Citation Analysis

23 references. Self-citations: 3 (refs 12, 13, 19, 20) = 17% SCR. Within acceptable range. Self-citations are relevant prior graphene work by same group.

## Structural Analysis

## Conclusion

paper.title: Effect of Silicon Surface Cleaning on Electrical Properties of As-Deposited Atomically Layer-Deposited (ALD) HfO2 Films Obtained From Tetrakis(dimethylamino)Hafnium (TDMAH) and Water
paper.abstract: Study of electrical properties of as-deposited ALD HfO2 films on Si-OH vs Si-H terminated silicon. C-V measurements show lower oxide trapped charge and higher dielectric constant on Si-OH surfaces. Weibull reliability analysis demonstrates improved reliability for Si-OH substrates.

verdict.summary: Solid semiconductor process study with appropriate formalism. Equations describe standard MOS physics: Mott-Schottky (Eq. 1), capacitance (Eq. 2), charge calculations (Eqs. 3-4), Weibull distribution (Eqs. 5-8). Chemical reaction mechanisms appropriately presented in text form.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Experimental study of surface cleaning effects on ALD HfO2 electrical properties
**Level:** 2-3 (Low-Medium)
**Rationale:** Applied materials science using standard MOS characterization techniques. Novel contribution is the systematic comparison of surface terminations.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 8 (Eqs. 1-8)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~16 (8 equations x 2)
Expected range (Level 2-3): 0-25
Ratio: 0.64x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - Standard MOS equations: Mott-Schottky (Eq. 1), Cox formula (Eq. 2), fixed charge (Eq. 3), trapped charge (Eq. 4), Weibull distribution (Eqs. 5-8).

### Necessity Test
- Eq. 1 (Mott-Schottky): Standard C-V analysis, necessary
- Eq. 2 (Capacitance): Required for dielectric constant extraction
- Eqs. 3-4 (Charge calculations): Necessary for electrical characterization
- Eqs. 5-8 (Weibull): Standard reliability analysis, necessary
- Chemical mechanisms shown in text (not equations): Appropriate choice

**Verdict:** Formalism appropriate for semiconductor materials study.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

17 references. Self-citations: 2 (refs 12, 13) = 12% SCR. Within acceptable range. Both self-citations are relevant prior work on same topic.

---

## Structural Analysis

Clear structure: theory, experimental methods, results by characterization type (morphological, electrical, reliability). Good data tables (Tables 1-3). Weibull plots provide statistical reliability analysis.

---

## Conclusion

Well-executed semiconductor process study. Mathematical formalism is standard MOS physics - no decoration. Good insight that Si-OH termination produces superior interface quality, explained through chemical reaction mechanisms.

paper.title: Technological aspects and simulation results for the Nothing On Insulator device developed into a planar technology
paper.abstract: Planar variant of Nothing On Insulator (NOI) tunneling device. Athena/Atlas simulations of technological flow and device functionality. Fowler-Nordheim tunneling as main conduction mechanism. Comparison with experimental MOSFET and MIM structures.

verdict.summary: Novel device concept paper with minimal formalism. Focuses on technology simulation and fabrication. No unnecessary mathematical notation - relies on Silvaco simulation tool for device physics. Appropriate for technology development paper.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Novel planar tunneling transistor technology development
**Level:** 2 (Low)
**Rationale:** Technology simulation and fabrication study. Device physics handled by Silvaco tools (Athena/Atlas). Paper contribution is process flow and device concept.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 0 formal numbered equations

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: 0
Expected range (Level 2): 0-15
Ratio: 0x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - Paper appropriately delegates device physics to simulation tools. Focus is on technological implementation.

### Necessity Test
N/A - No formal elements beyond simulation tool usage.

**Verdict:** Appropriate minimal formalism for technology development paper.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

18 references. Self-citations: 7 (refs 4, 7-10, 13, 16) = 39% SCR. **Elevated.** However, all self-citations are directly relevant to NOI device development by same research group. This represents a focused research program rather than citation padding.

---

## Structural Analysis

Simulation methodology well-documented. Fabrication steps presented. Experimental validation limited to oxide characterization (ellipsometry, AFM). Comparison with external experimental data (MIM, MOSFET) provides validation.

---

## Conclusion

Technology-focused paper with appropriate minimal formalism. High self-citation rate reflects focused research program on NOI devices. Main value is demonstration of planar NOI feasibility and process flow documentation.

paper.title: Galvanically Isolated IGBT Gate Driver with Advanced Protections and A Fault Detection and Reporting Method
paper.abstract: IGBT gate driver with UVLO, desaturation detection, soft shutdown, and Active Miller Clamp protections. Fault encoding method for transmission across galvanic barrier. Implemented in 0.25um CMOS BCD technology with experimental validation.

verdict.summary: Well-executed circuit design paper with appropriate formalism. Equations describe standard circuit relationships (Eqs. 1-7): power dissipation, voltage/current relationships, blanking time calculation. All equations are functional and necessary for design. Experimental validation confirms simulation.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** IGBT gate driver circuit design with fault detection and protection functions
**Level:** 3 (Medium)
**Rationale:** Novel circuit architecture for gate driver with multiple protection functions and fault encoding scheme. Requires circuit analysis equations.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 7 (Eqs. 1-7)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~14 (7 equations x 2)
Expected range (Level 3): 10-40
Ratio: 0.35x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - Equations are functional circuit relationships: conduction loss (Eq. 1), DESAT voltage (Eq. 2), blanking time (Eqs. 3-4), Miller current (Eqs. 5-7). Each serves design purpose.

### Necessity Test
- Eq. 1 (PCON): Power dissipation, necessary for design
- Eqs. 2-4 (DESAT): Required for protection circuit sizing
- Eqs. 5-7 (Miller clamp): Necessary for AMC design
- Would removing change implementation? YES - equations determine component values

**Verdict:** Formalism necessary for circuit design.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

22 references. Self-citations: 0 = 0% SCR. Excellent. References cover relevant prior work on gate drivers, isolation, and IGBT protection.

---

## Structural Analysis

Excellent structure: principle of operation, conceptual design, simulation, experimental validation. Oscilloscope captures (Figs. 10-15) validate all protection functions. Fabricated in real technology (0.25um CMOS BCD).

---

## Conclusion

Strong circuit design paper. Equations serve functional purpose (component sizing, protection timing). Complete design flow from concept through fabrication and validation. Zero self-citations indicates mature, externally validated research area.

paper.title: Efficiency Optimizations for Koller and Sahami's Feature Selection Algorithm
paper.abstract: Four optimizations for Koller and Sahami's Feature Selection algorithm: Gamma Decomposition, Removed Features Database, In-iteration Parallelism, and Iteration Cache. Reduces algorithm runtime from 22 hours to 57 minutes (4.36% of original).

verdict.summary: Excellent algorithm optimization paper with appropriate formalism. Information theory equations (Eqs. 1-18) are necessary for describing the Gamma Decomposition optimization. Key contribution is proving conditional mutual information can be decomposed into entropy terms. Code publicly available.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Algorithm optimization for feature selection using information theory
**Level:** 3 (Medium)
**Rationale:** Novel optimization of existing algorithm. Gamma Decomposition requires proving mathematical equivalence of information-theoretic quantities.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 formal numbered definitions
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 1 (implicit - Gamma Decomposition derivation)
- Equations: 18 (Eqs. 1-18)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~40 (18 equations x 2 + proof)
Expected range (Level 3): 10-40
Ratio: 1.0x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - Equations define information-theoretic concepts: Kullback-Leibler divergence (Eqs. 1-3), mutual information (Eqs. 8-11), algorithm definition (Eqs. 4-6), Gamma decomposition (Eqs. 12-18).

### Necessity Test
- Eqs. 1-3 (KL divergence): Necessary - foundation for algorithm heuristic
- Eqs. 4-6 (Algorithm definition): Required for formal specification
- Eqs. 8-11 (Mutual information): Necessary for decomposition proof
- Eqs. 12-18 (Gamma decomposition): Core contribution - proves mathematical equivalence enabling optimization
- Would removing change implementation? YES - decomposition is the main optimization

**Verdict:** Formalism necessary for algorithm optimization proof.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

17 references. Self-citations: 1 (ref 2) = 6% SCR. Low and appropriate. Good coverage of Markov blanket feature selection literature.

---

## Structural Analysis

Excellent structure: background, related work, algorithm definition, each optimization with experimental evaluation. Tables 1-3 provide clear performance comparisons. Code published under GPL 3 license [16].

---

## Conclusion

Strong algorithm optimization paper. Information theory formalism is necessary for proving the Gamma Decomposition. Significant speedup (22+ hours to 57 minutes) demonstrated with experimental evaluation. Open-source implementation adds reproducibility value.

batch_notes: Volume 22 Issue 1 (2019) analyzed with RUIN Framework v2.0 and Necessity Test. **No formalism theater detected.** All 7 papers have domain-appropriate formalism: theoretical plasmonics (high formalism, justified), semiconductor device physics (standard physics equations), materials characterization (minimal formalism), technology simulation (minimal formalism), circuit design (functional equations), and algorithm optimization (information theory). Final: 0 CRITICAL, 0 LIMITED, 5 ADEQUATE, 2 STRONG.
